title: Alternative adiabatic quantum dynamics with algorithmic applications
abstract: In adiabatic quantum computing the aim is to track an eigenstate as the Hamiltonian changes. In the usual setup this is achieved using the natural time-dependent Hamiltonian evolution of the system and the main technical tool is the adiabatic theorem. We propose several alternative processes that achieve the same goal, but can easily be implemented on a gate-based quantum computer without the overhead of simulating time-dependent Hamiltonian evolution. We give a general framework for deriving `adiabatic' theorems for these processes.   As an application, we give various algorithms for solving the Quantum Linear Systems Problem (QLSP) with optimal scaling in the condition number. One of these algorithms was previously developed in [Cunningham, Roland 2024] and another can be seen as a randomised version of the discrete adiabatic algorithm of [Costa et al. 2022].   We also describe versions of Trotterisation in our framework, which allows several results from [An et al. 2025] to be reproduced in a randomised setting. In particular, bounds on the Trotter error in terms of the fidelity are obtained that are asymptotically better than the standard bounds.
summary: This paper presents a comprehensive theoretical framework for analyzing adiabatic quantum dynamics, proposing methods that bypass the need to simulate time-dependent Hamiltonians directly. By developing generalized adiabatic theorems for processes like randomisation and adapted scheduling, the authors achieve optimal scaling for key quantum algorithms such as QLSP. This advances the theoretical understanding of fault-tolerant quantum computation protocols.
main_problem: The paper develops alternative theoretical frameworks to achieve the goal of adiabatic quantum computing—tracking an eigenstate as a Hamiltonian changes—without incurring the computational overhead of simulating time-dependent Hamiltonian evolution.
main_result: It establishes general theorems and provides concrete algorithms for achieving optimal scaling in solving problems like the Quantum Linear Systems Problem (QLSP) by utilizing techniques like Poisson-distributed randomisation and adapted time schedules.
method: The methodology involves deriving 'adiabatic' theorems for non-standard evolution processes, employing spectral theory, contour integration, and analyzing the fidelity bounds derived from master equations or generalized differential equations.
model_or_system: The core model is a quantum system governed by a time-dependent Hamiltonian H(s) evolving from an initial state to a final eigenstate. The analysis extends to unitaries generated via Poisson processes or Trotter approximations.
key_observables: Infidelity ($1 - 	ext{Tr}(P(1)ho(1))$), Fidelity ($	ext{Tr}(ho(1)P(1))$), and the spectral gap $g(s)$.
important_parameters: The evolution time $T$, the gap function $g(s)$, and the condition number $\kappa$ (for QLSP).
main_assumptions: Assumptions include the differentiability of Hamiltonians, the existence of spectral projectors, and the ability to define appropriate integration contours in the complex plane.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the spectrum of the Hamiltonian, the necessity of adapted time schedules due to non-constant gaps, and the integration contours used in the mathematical proofs.
paper_structure: The paper builds from general frameworks for deriving adiabatic theorems (Theorem 1) to specific implementations using adapted schedules (Theorem 4), stochastic processes (Poisson unitaries, Theorem 6), and finally applies these tools to optimize algorithms like QLSP and Grover search.
why_interesting: The work provides rigorous, mathematically advanced tools to analyze quantum computation protocols, offering alternative, potentially more efficient, pathways than direct time evolution simulation for state preparation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
